text: The patient is positioned in the right lateral position for the robotic-assisted espermectomy. The surgeon uses robotic arms to lift the spleen and retract the hilum, carefully dissecting the peritoneal attachments layer by layer to access the splenic artery. Fluorescent technology is employed to visualize and precisely isolate the vessels, ensuring safe dissection around the splenic vessels while avoiding the tip of the pancreas.
The surgeon clips and cuts the splenic vessels individually after they have been isolated.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to quickly and bloodlessly detach the gastroesophageal ligaments containing the short gastric vessels from the spleen. The lateral peritoneal attachments, including those to the colon and ligaments, are also protected during this process. After the spleen is completely resected, the splenic bed is irrigated to confirm hemostasis, and a T-cell bio-glue is applied to enhance hemostasis. Finally, the spleen is placed into a large bag for removal.
